clinical_history: A 37 yr old male with a history of an abnormal ECG is referred for further evaluation. He has no cardiac symptoms. On exam, blood pressure is normal and a soft systolic murmur is heard, unchanged with Valsalva maneuver or standing. The ECG is most consistent with which ONE of the following diagnoses?
diagnosis: The ECG shows sinus rhythm at 70/min with normal intervals and a normal QRS axis of about 25 degrees. Notable features are very prominent QRS voltages with deep, spade-like T wave inversions in leads I, II, aVF, aVL, V3-V6, and ST depressions, especially in the mid-lateral precordial leads. These findings are typical of the apical variant of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, also called Yamaguchis syndrome. This diagnosis was confirmed by transthoracic echocardiography. The depth of the T wave inversions distinguishes this ECG from more common LVH variants. The very prominent voltages and, especially the ST depressions distinguish it from physiologic athletes ECGs. See also Case 14 and Eur. Heart J 2010: 31:243.
